questions.question:
Consider a demand paging memory management system with 32-bit logical address, 20-bit physical address, and page size of 2^10 bytes. Assuming that the memory is byte addressable, what is the maximum number of entries in the page table?

A. 2^10
B. 2^20
C. 2^22
D. 2^32
questions.to_find: Maximum number of entries in the page table

understand.plain: A demand paging system uses a page table to map logical pages to physical frames. The number of entries in the page table equals the number of pages in the logical address space. With a 32-bit logical address and page size of 2^10 bytes, we can find how many pages the process can have.

keywords.explain: The total addressable memory from the process's perspective, determined by the number of bits in the logical address.
keywords.example: With 32-bit logical address, logical address space = 2^32 bytes.

keywords.explain: The fixed-size chunk into which both logical and physical memory are divided. Page size = 2^(page offset bits).
keywords.example: Page size 2^10 bytes means 10 bits are used for the offset within a page.

keywords.explain: Each entry corresponds to one logical page. The number of entries = total logical address space / page size.

understand.visual_alt: Logical address split: 32 bits total = 22 bits (page number) + 10 bits (page offset). Number of page table entries = 2^22.

given.aim: Find the number of entries in the page table.

terms.meaning: The process can address 2^32 bytes of logical memory.
terms.example: A 32-bit CPU can address up to 4 GB of logical space.
terms.connects: Determines total logical address space, which sets how many pages exist.

terms.meaning: Each page (and frame) is 1024 bytes = 1 KB.
terms.example: Page offset needs 10 bits to address any byte within a page.
terms.connects: Page offset bits = 10; remaining bits = page number bits.
Physical address size is given but does NOT affect the number of page table entries — that depends only on the logical address space.

given.plan: Number of page table entries = (Total logical address space) / (Page size) = 2^32 / 2^10 = 2^22. The page number field in the logical address has 32 - 10 = 22 bits, so there are 2^22 possible pages, requiring 2^22 page table entries.

steps.title: Identify the page offset bits
steps.apply: Page size = 2^10 bytes, so page offset bits = 10.
steps.note: The page offset bits tell you how many bits of the logical address are used to index within a page.

steps.title: Compute the page number bits
steps.note: These 22 bits form the index into the page table.

steps.title: Compute the number of page table entries
steps.note: The physical address size (20 bits) is irrelevant to the number of entries; it only affects how wide each entry is (frame number = 20 - 10 = 10 bits).

solution.result: The maximum number of entries in the page table is 2^22. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Number of page table entries = 2^(logical_address_bits - page_offset_bits) = 2^(32-10) = 2^22.

questions.question:
Which of the following actions is/are typically NOT performed by the operating system when switching context from process A to process B?

A. Saving current register values and restoring saved register values for process B.
B. Changing address translation tables.
C. Swapping out the memory image of process A to the disk.
D. Invalidating the translation look-aside buffer.
questions.answer: C. Swapping out the memory image of process A to the disk.
questions.to_find: Which action is NOT typically performed during a context switch

understand.plain: A context switch is the OS mechanism to pause one process and resume another. It involves saving the current process state and loading the next process's state. However, swapping a process entirely out to disk is a much heavier operation (swapping/paging out) that is NOT part of a normal context switch.

keywords.explain: The OS saves the CPU state (registers, PC, etc.) of the running process and loads the saved state of the next process to be run.
keywords.example: Switching from process A (paused at instruction 100) to process B (which was at instruction 250 when last paused).

keywords.explain: A hardware cache that speeds up virtual-to-physical address translation. On a context switch, it is typically invalidated (flushed) because the new process has different address mappings.
keywords.example: After switching from process A to process B, TLB entries for A's pages are no longer valid for B.

keywords.explain: Moving an entire process image from RAM to disk to free memory. This is a separate, expensive operation, not part of every context switch.
keywords.example: An OS may swap out a sleeping process if memory is scarce, but not on every context switch.

understand.visual_alt: Context switch timeline: Process A runs -> save A's registers & state -> update page tables/TLB -> restore B's state -> Process B runs. Swapping A to disk is not in this path.

given.aim: Identify which action among the options is NOT a standard part of context switching.

terms.meaning: The OS saves the CPU register values (PC, SP, general-purpose registers) of the outgoing process and loads the saved register values of the incoming process.
terms.example: PCB (Process Control Block) stores these values.
terms.connects: This IS performed — it is the core of a context switch.

terms.meaning: The page table base register (e.g., CR3 on x86) is updated to point to the new process's page table.
terms.example: Without updating CR3, process B would use process A's address mappings.
terms.connects: This IS performed — required for memory protection and correctness.

terms.term: Swapping out memory image to disk (Option C)
terms.meaning: Writing the entire memory image of a process from RAM to secondary storage.
terms.example: Swapping is done by a memory manager/swapper, not on every context switch.
terms.connects: This is NOT performed on a routine context switch — too expensive and not necessary.

terms.meaning: Flushing the TLB to remove stale address translation entries belonging to the previous process.
terms.example: On x86, loading a new CR3 automatically flushes the TLB.
terms.connects: This IS performed — needed to prevent process B from using process A's cached translations.

given.plan: Evaluate each option: A, B, D are standard context-switch activities. Option C (swapping out to disk) is a heavyweight operation done by the swapper/pager only under memory pressure, not on every context switch.

steps.title: Analyze what a context switch must do
steps.apply: A context switch must: (1) save CPU state of process A, (2) update memory management structures (page table base register), (3) flush/invalidate TLB, (4) restore CPU state of process B. All of A, B, D describe these mandatory steps.
steps.note: These steps preserve correctness: registers must be right for resumption, address mappings must match the new process, TLB must not serve stale entries.

steps.title: Check Option C
steps.apply: Swapping out process A's entire memory image to disk requires I/O operations and is orders of magnitude slower than a context switch. This is performed only by the memory manager (swapper) when physical memory is under pressure, not as a routine part of every context switch.
steps.note: A context switch can happen hundreds of times per second; disk I/O would make this infeasible if swapping were part of every switch.

solution.result: Option C — Swapping out the memory image of process A to the disk — is NOT typically performed during a context switch.

questions.formula_note: Context switch saves/restores PCB state (registers, page table pointer), flushes TLB. Swapping (disk I/O) is a separate OS activity, not part of every context switch.

questions.question:
Which of the following need not necessarily be saved on a context switch between processes?

A. General purpose registers
B. Translation look-aside buffer
C. Program counter
D. All of the above
questions.to_find: Which component does NOT need to be saved during a context switch

understand.plain: During a context switch, the OS saves the state of the current process so it can be resumed later. The program counter and general-purpose registers must be saved because they represent exactly where the process is and what data it was working with. The TLB, however, is a hardware cache shared by all processes — it does not belong to any single process and does not need to be saved; it is simply invalidated (flushed).

keywords.explain: CPU registers (e.g., EAX, EBX on x86) that hold values the currently running process is computing with. These must be saved per-process.
keywords.example: If process A has EAX = 42, that value must be saved so it is restored when A runs again.

keywords.explain: A hardware associative cache for address translations. It is not part of any process's state; its contents are hardware-managed and are just flushed on a context switch.
keywords.example: TLB is analogous to a CPU cache — you don't 'save' a cache; you invalidate it.

keywords.explain: Holds the address of the next instruction to execute for the current process. Must be saved to resume the process correctly.
keywords.example: If process A was at instruction address 0x1004, the PC must be saved as 0x1004 in A's PCB.

understand.visual_alt: PCB contents: PC, registers, stack pointer, memory map. TLB is a shared hardware cache — flushed not saved.

given.aim: Identify which item does not need to be saved in the PCB during a context switch.

terms.meaning: Per-process CPU working registers; different processes have independent register sets.
terms.example: Two processes can both have EAX, but with different values.
terms.connects: Must be saved — otherwise process state is lost.

terms.meaning: Shared hardware cache for address translations; not owned by any process.
terms.example: When process B runs, TLB entries from process A would cause incorrect translations.
terms.connects: Need NOT be saved — just invalidated/flushed on each context switch.

terms.meaning: Tracks where each process is in its execution.
terms.example: Process A paused at address 0x2000; PC must be saved as 0x2000.
terms.connects: Must be saved — essential to resume process at the right instruction.

given.plan: The TLB is a hardware structure that caches address translations; it belongs to no single process. On a context switch it is flushed, not saved. All other CPU state (registers, PC, etc.) is process-specific and must be saved in the PCB.

steps.title: Identify per-process state
steps.apply: General purpose registers and the program counter are part of a process's execution context — they change as the process runs and are unique to each process. They MUST be saved in the PCB (Process Control Block) during a context switch.
steps.note: Other saved items typically include the stack pointer, status register (flags), and the page-table base register.

steps.title: Analyze the TLB
steps.apply: The TLB is a hardware cache shared across all processes. Saving TLB contents makes no sense because: (1) after a context switch the new process needs its own address translations, not the previous process's; (2) the correct action is to flush/invalidate the TLB so stale entries do not misdirect the new process's memory accesses.
steps.note: Modern architectures like x86 automatically flush the TLB when CR3 (page directory base) is loaded with a new value during a context switch.

solution.result: The TLB need not necessarily be saved on a context switch. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: TLB is a shared hardware cache — flushed (not saved) on context switch. Per-process CPU state (PC, registers) is saved in the PCB.

questions.question:
Let the time taken to switch from user mode to kernel mode of execution be T1, while time taken to switch between two user processes be T2. Which of the following is correct?

A. T1 > T2
B. T1 = T2
C. T1 < T2
D. Nothing can be said about the relation between T1 and T2
questions.to_find: The relationship between time to switch from user mode to kernel mode (T1) and time to switch between two user processes (T2)

understand.plain: Switching from user mode to kernel mode (T1) is a mode switch — it only requires saving/restoring a few registers and changing the privilege level. Switching between two user processes (T2) is a full context switch — it requires saving the entire state of the outgoing process, updating page tables and TLB, and loading the saved state of the incoming process. Since a full context switch does far more work than a mode switch, T2 > T1.

keywords.explain: Changing the CPU privilege level from user mode to kernel mode (e.g., via a system call or interrupt). Only the minimum CPU state is saved; the same process continues in kernel mode.
keywords.example: A system call saves the user-mode PC and stack pointer, switches to kernel stack, and enters kernel mode.

keywords.explain: Replacing the CPU context entirely: saving all registers, PC, page table pointer, flushing TLB, and loading the new process's full state.
keywords.example: Switching from Process A to Process B involves updating CR3, flushing the TLB, saving A's PCB, and restoring B's PCB.

understand.visual_alt: Mode switch (T1): user->kernel, same process, light operation. Context switch (T2): process A->process B, full state save/restore + page table switch + TLB flush, heavier operation. T1 < T2.

given.aim: Compare T1 (user-to-kernel mode switch time) with T2 (process-to-process context switch time).

terms.term: T1 = time for user mode to kernel mode switch
terms.meaning: Time for a mode switch within the SAME process — e.g., when a system call is issued.
terms.example: The process issues an int 0x80 instruction; CPU saves a few registers, changes privilege level.
terms.connects: Involves minimal state saving — just the user-mode PC, stack pointer, and flags.

terms.term: T2 = time for context switch between two user processes
terms.meaning: Time to pause process A and resume process B, involving full state save/restore.
terms.example: Timer interrupt causes scheduler to preempt A and schedule B; full PCB save and restore occurs.
terms.connects: Involves saving all registers, changing page tables, flushing TLB — much more than T1.

terms.term: Context switch is a superset of mode switch
terms.meaning: A process context switch includes a mode switch (kernel mode is needed to do the switch) plus many additional steps.
terms.example: During a context switch, the OS first enters kernel mode (T1 cost), then does additional work to switch processes.
terms.connects: Since context switch includes mode switch and more, T2 > T1, i.e., T1 < T2.

given.plan: A mode switch (user->kernel) involves only a privilege level change and saving of a few registers. A process context switch involves a mode switch PLUS saving/restoring the full CPU state, switching page tables, and flushing the TLB. Therefore T2 > T1, meaning T1 < T2.

steps.title: Analyze the user-to-kernel mode switch (T1)
steps.apply: A user-to-kernel mode switch occurs on a system call or interrupt. The same process continues to execute, now in kernel mode. Steps: (1) save user-mode PC and stack pointer, (2) switch to kernel stack, (3) change CPU privilege level. This is a lightweight operation with very few steps.
steps.note: The process identity does not change; only the execution mode changes.

steps.title: Analyze the process-to-process context switch (T2)
steps.apply: A full context switch between two user processes requires: (1) entering kernel mode (this already includes T1's cost), (2) saving ALL CPU registers and PC of process A into its PCB, (3) updating the page table base register to point to process B's page table, (4) flushing the TLB, (5) restoring all registers and PC from process B's PCB. This is a strictly heavier operation than a mode switch.
steps.note: T2 includes T1 as a subset, plus many more steps. Therefore T2 > T1.

steps.title: Conclude the relationship
steps.apply: Since a process context switch requires all the work of a mode switch plus additional register saves, page table updates, and TLB flushes: T1 < T2.

questions.formula_note: A process context switch is a superset of a mode switch; it includes all mode-switch work plus full CPU state save/restore and memory management updates. Hence T1 (mode switch) < T2 (context switch).

questions.question:
Consider a process P running on a CPU. Which one or more of the following events will ALWAYS trigger a context switch by the OS that results in process P moving to a non-running state (e.g., ready, blocked)?

A. P makes a system call to read data from a disk
B. A timer interrupt occurs
C. P makes a system call to write data to a pipe with a full buffer
D. An interrupt is raised by a network interface card (NIC)
questions.to_find: Which events ALWAYS cause process P to move to a non-running (ready or blocked) state

understand.plain: Not every interrupt or system call causes the currently running process to be descheduled. We need to identify which events ALWAYS result in process P leaving the running state. Disk reads always block the process (disk I/O is slow, so the OS puts P to sleep waiting for the data). Writing to a full pipe also always blocks (the pipe buffer is full, so P must wait). A timer interrupt may or may not cause a context switch depending on scheduling policy. An NIC interrupt is handled by the kernel but P is usually resumed immediately afterwards if no higher-priority process is ready.

keywords.explain: A system call that cannot complete immediately and causes the calling process to be suspended (moved to blocked state) until the resource is available.
keywords.example: read() from disk: disk I/O is always slower than CPU, so the process blocks until data arrives.

keywords.explain: A periodic interrupt generated by the hardware timer, used by the OS scheduler to preempt processes. Whether it causes a context switch depends on the scheduling algorithm and whether a higher-priority process is ready.
keywords.example: Under FCFS scheduling, a timer interrupt may not preempt the current process.

keywords.explain: When a process writes to a pipe whose buffer is completely full, the write blocks until some space is freed by a reader process.
keywords.example: write() to a full pipe: the OS always suspends P until the reader drains some data.

understand.visual_alt: Event analysis: disk read (A) -> always blocks P; timer interrupt (B) -> may or may not preempt P; full pipe write (C) -> always blocks P; NIC interrupt (D) -> handled by OS, P usually resumes.

given.aim: Determine which events ALWAYS move process P out of the running state.

terms.term: Option A: P reads from disk
terms.meaning: Disk I/O is a blocking operation — the device is vastly slower than the CPU, and data is never immediately available.
terms.example: read(fd, buf, size) on a disk file always results in an I/O wait; P moves to BLOCKED.
terms.connects: This ALWAYS triggers a context switch — P always blocks waiting for disk.

terms.meaning: A periodic hardware interrupt that notifies the OS that a time quantum may have expired. The OS may or may not preempt P.
terms.example: Under non-preemptive FCFS, the timer fires but the OS does not switch away from P.
terms.connects: Does NOT always cause a context switch — depends on scheduler policy and whether a higher-priority process is ready.

terms.term: Option C: Write to full pipe
terms.meaning: If the pipe buffer is completely full, the writing process is always blocked until the reader consumes some data.
terms.example: write() to a full pipe: POSIX guarantees this blocks.
terms.connects: This ALWAYS triggers a context switch — P always blocks on a full pipe.

terms.meaning: An interrupt from the network interface card signals incoming data or completion of a send. The OS handles it in an interrupt service routine, but the interrupted process P is typically resumed afterwards if no other process is ready.
terms.example: A network packet arrives; the ISR runs, then P continues — no guarantee P is descheduled.
terms.connects: Does NOT always cause a context switch away from P.

given.plan: Identify blocking vs. non-blocking events. A disk read (A) is always a blocking I/O operation. Writing to a full pipe (C) always blocks. A timer interrupt (B) and NIC interrupt (D) may or may not cause a context switch depending on the scheduling policy and system state.

steps.title: Check Option A: Disk read system call
steps.apply: A disk read is an I/O-bound blocking system call. The disk device takes milliseconds to deliver data; the CPU cannot wait idly. The OS always moves P to the BLOCKED state and schedules another process. When the disk interrupt arrives later, P is moved back to READY.
steps.note: Disk reads ALWAYS block the calling process — no exception.

steps.title: Check Option B: Timer interrupt
steps.apply: A timer interrupt gives the OS scheduler a chance to preempt P, but whether it does depends on the scheduling policy. Under non-preemptive FCFS or if P has the highest priority, P continues to run. Thus a timer interrupt does NOT always cause a context switch.
steps.note: Timer interrupt is necessary for preemption but not sufficient to guarantee a switch.

steps.title: Check Option C: Write to full pipe
steps.apply: POSIX semantics guarantee that writing to a full pipe blocks the writer. The pipe buffer is full; P cannot complete the write without a reader consuming data. The OS always moves P to BLOCKED until space is available.
steps.note: Full pipe write ALWAYS blocks the writing process — no exception.

steps.title: Check Option D: NIC interrupt
steps.apply: An NIC interrupt is handled by the OS ISR. After handling, if no higher-priority process is ready to run, P resumes execution. P is NOT necessarily moved to a non-running state.
steps.note: An NIC interrupt may cause a context switch if it wakes a higher-priority process, but does not ALWAYS do so.

solution.result: Options A and C ALWAYS trigger a context switch that moves P to a non-running state. Answer: A, C.

questions.formula_note: Blocking I/O system calls (disk read, write to full pipe) always force the calling process to the BLOCKED state. Timer and hardware interrupts may or may not cause a context switch depending on scheduler policy and system state.
